meta.title: Unit 2 — Common & Proper Nouns
meta.subject: English Grammar
meta.description: Learn to tell the difference between common nouns and proper nouns.

questions.question: Which word is a proper noun?
questions.explanation: "Taiwan" is a proper noun because it names a specific country. Proper nouns start with a capital letter.

questions.question: "teacher" is a proper noun.
questions.explanation: "teacher" is a common noun — it refers to any teacher, not a specific person.

questions.question: Which word is a common noun?
questions.explanation: "school" is a common noun. Monday, Amy, and January are all proper nouns.

questions.question: Sort each word into Common Noun or Proper Noun:
questions.explanation: Taipei (city name) and Sunday (day of week) are proper nouns. cat, book, river are common nouns.

questions.question: Proper nouns always begin with a capital letter.
questions.explanation: Yes! Proper nouns (names of specific people, places, days, months) always start with a capital letter.

questions.question: The name of our country is ___ (taiwan / Taiwan).
questions.hint: Proper nouns start with a capital letter
questions.explanation: Taiwan is a proper noun (a specific country name), so it must start with a capital T.

questions.question: Which sentence has correct capitalization?
questions.explanation: "I live in Taipei." — "I" is always capitalized, and "Taipei" is a proper noun.

questions.question: Sort the words — Common Noun or Proper Noun?
questions.explanation: March (month name) and Disneyland (place name) are proper nouns.

questions.question: "ocean" is a proper noun because oceans are big and important.
questions.explanation: "ocean" is a common noun. However, "Pacific Ocean" would be a proper noun because it names a specific ocean.

questions.question: My friend's name is ___ (amy / Amy).
questions.explanation: Amy is a proper noun (a person's name), so it starts with a capital letter.

questions.question: Arrange the words to form a correct sentence:
How many nouns are in this sentence? "The cat sat on the mat."
